title: GitHub Review by greptile-apps[bot]
summary:
**Review Summary:**

The reviewer identified 5 issues across the Keystone operator, including one silent correctness bug. (1) The `Watches(Secret)` with `EnqueueRequestForOwner` won't trigger for ESO-managed secrets since their owner is `ExternalSecret`, not `Keystone` — either remove the watch or use `EnqueueRequestsFromMapFunc` with a namespace lookup. (2) All sub-reconcilers hardcode resource names like `"keystone"`, `"keystone-api"`, etc., which will conflict if multiple Keystone CRs exist in the same namespace — names should incorporate the CR name (e.g., `fmt.Sprintf("%s-db-sync", keystone.Name)`). (3) The bootstrap job sets admin, internal, and public endpoint URLs to the same cluster-local address, making the public endpoint unreachable for external clients — add a `PublicEndpoint` field to `BootstrapSpec`. (4) `reconcileSecrets` only checks ESO sync status but skips the `secrets.IsSecretReady` verification described in the PR, risking a race where the materialized Secret isn't yet in etcd when downstream reconcilers read it. (5) **Critical bug:** the Fernet rotation CronJob runs `fernet_rotate` against a tmpfs-backed volume mount, so rotated keys are never persisted back to the Kubernetes Secret — after pod restart, keys revert and tokens signed under rotated keys cause 401s; the job must write updated keys back via the Kubernetes API.

code_comments.body:
**`Watches(Secret)` with `EnqueueRequestForOwner` will not trigger for ESO-managed secrets**

The purpose of this `Watches` declaration is described in the PR as re-enqueuing the Keystone reconciler when ESO syncs the DB-credentials or admin-password secrets. However, `handler.EnqueueRequestForOwner` with `handler.OnlyControllerOwner()` only fires when the changed object's `ownerReferences` contains a controller reference pointing to a `Keystone` CR.

ESO-managed secrets (`spec.database.secretRef`, `spec.bootstrap.adminPasswordSecretRef`) are created and owned by the `ExternalSecret` controller — their `ownerReferences[].kind` is `ExternalSecret`, not `Keystone`. So these secrets will never match the owner filter, and the corresponding Keystone reconcile will never be enqueued reactively.

The `reconcileSecrets` 15-second requeue still works as a safety net, but the watch declaration gives the false impression of event-driven behaviour. Either:
- Remove the watch and rely explicitly on the polling requeue, or
- Replace `EnqueueRequestForOwner` with `handler.EnqueueRequestsFromMapFunc`, implementing a lookup of which `Keystone` CR in the same namespace references the changed secret by name.

<details><summary>Prompt To Fix With AI</summary>

`````markdown
This is a comment left during a code review.
Path: operators/keystone/internal/controller/keystone_controller.go
Line: 145

Comment:
**`Watches(Secret)` with `EnqueueRequestForOwner` will not trigger for ESO-managed secrets**

The purpose of this `Watches` declaration is described in the PR as re-enqueuing the Keystone reconciler when ESO syncs the DB-credentials or admin-password secrets. However, `handler.EnqueueRequestForOwner` with `handler.OnlyControllerOwner()` only fires when the changed object's `ownerReferences` contains a controller reference pointing to a `Keystone` CR.

ESO-managed secrets (`spec.database.secretRef`, `spec.bootstrap.adminPasswordSecretRef`) are created and owned by the `ExternalSecret` controller — their `ownerReferences[].kind` is `ExternalSecret`, not `Keystone`. So these secrets will never match the owner filter, and the corresponding Keystone reconcile will never be enqueued reactively.

The `reconcileSecrets` 15-second requeue still works as a safety net, but the watch declaration gives the false impression of event-driven behaviour. Either:
- Remove the watch and rely explicitly on the polling requeue, or
- Replace `EnqueueRequestForOwner` with `handler.EnqueueRequestsFromMapFunc`, implementing a lookup of which `Keystone` CR in the same namespace references the changed secret by name.

How can I resolve this? If you propose a fix, please make it concise.
`````
</details>
code_comments.author: greptile-apps[bot]
code_comments.resolution: [0] Replaced handler.EnqueueRequestForOwner with handler.EnqueueRequestsFromMapFunc(secretToKeystoneMapper(mgr.GetClient())) in SetupWithManager. The new secretToKeystoneMapper function lists all Keystone CRs in the changed Secret's namespace and enqueues reconcile requests for any CR whose spec.database.secretRef.name or spec.bootstrap.adminPasswordSecretRef.name matches the Secret name, or whose UID appears in the Secret's ownerReferences. An isOwnedBy helper was added to support the ownership check.

code_comments.body:
**Hardcoded resource names conflict when multiple Keystone CRs exist in the same namespace**

`buildDatabase`, `buildUser`, `buildGrant`, and `buildDBSyncJob` all hard-code the name `"keystone"` for their managed resources. The same pattern appears in `reconcile_deployment.go` (`"keystone-api"`, `"keystone-api"` service), `reconcile_fernet.go` (`"keystone-fernet-keys"`, `"keystone-fernet-rotate"`, `"keystone-fernet-keys-backup"`), and `reconcile_bootstrap.go` (`"keystone-bootstrap"`).

If a second `Keystone` CR is created in the same namespace (e.g., for a staging vs. production environment, or a canary deployment), both reconcilers will race to own resources with the same name. The first reconciler's owner reference will win; the second will get an `AlreadyExists` error or silently operate against the wrong resources.

Resource names should incorporate the `Keystone` CR name, e.g.:

```go
Name: fmt.Sprintf("%s-db-sync", keystone.Name),
```

This applies consistently to all resources created by all sub-reconcilers:
- `reconcile_database.go`: `"keystone"` (Database, User, Grant), `"keystone-db-sync"` (Job)
- `reconcile_deployment.go`: `"keystone-api"` (Deployment, Service)
- `reconcile_fernet.go`: `"keystone-fernet-keys"` (Secret, CronJob, PushSecret), `"keystone-fernet-keys-backup"` (PushSecret)
- `reconcile_bootstrap.go`: `"keystone-bootstrap"` (Job)

<details><summary>Prompt To Fix With AI</summary>

`````markdown
This is a comment left during a code review.
Path: operators/keystone/internal/controller/reconcile_database.go
Line: 103-119

Comment:
**Hardcoded resource names conflict when multiple Keystone CRs exist in the same namespace**

`buildDatabase`, `buildUser`, `buildGrant`, and `buildDBSyncJob` all hard-code the name `"keystone"` for their managed resources. The same pattern appears in `reconcile_deployment.go` (`"keystone-api"`, `"keystone-api"` service), `reconcile_fernet.go` (`"keystone-fernet-keys"`, `"keystone-fernet-rotate"`, `"keystone-fernet-keys-backup"`), and `reconcile_bootstrap.go` (`"keystone-bootstrap"`).

If a second `Keystone` CR is created in the same namespace (e.g., for a staging vs. production environment, or a canary deployment), both reconcilers will race to own resources with the same name. The first reconciler's owner reference will win; the second will get an `AlreadyExists` error or silently operate against the wrong resources.

Resource names should incorporate the `Keystone` CR name, e.g.:

```go
Name: fmt.Sprintf("%s-db-sync", keystone.Name),
```

This applies consistently to all resources created by all sub-reconcilers:
- `reconcile_database.go`: `"keystone"` (Database, User, Grant), `"keystone-db-sync"` (Job)
- `reconcile_deployment.go`: `"keystone-api"` (Deployment, Service)
- `reconcile_fernet.go`: `"keystone-fernet-keys"` (Secret, CronJob, PushSecret), `"keystone-fernet-keys-backup"` (PushSecret)
- `reconcile_bootstrap.go`: `"keystone-bootstrap"` (Job)

How can I resolve this? If you propose a fix, please make it concise.
`````
</details>
code_comments.author: greptile-apps[bot]
code_comments.resolution: [1] All hardcoded resource names across all sub-reconcilers now incorporate keystone.Name: reconcile_database.go uses keystone.Name for Database/User/Grant names and fmt.Sprintf("%s-db-sync", keystone.Name) for the Job; reconcile_deployment.go uses fmt.Sprintf("%s-api", keystone.Name) for Deployment and Service; reconcile_fernet.go uses fmt.Sprintf("%s-fernet-keys", keystone.Name) for the Secret, fmt.Sprintf("%s-fernet-rotate", keystone.Name) for the CronJob and ServiceAccount, and fmt.Sprintf("%s-fernet-keys-backup", keystone.Name) for the PushSecret; reconcile_bootstrap.go uses fmt.Sprintf("%s-bootstrap", keystone.Name) for the Job and fmt.Sprintf("%s-api", keystone.Name) in the service URL.

code_comments.body:
**All three bootstrap endpoint URLs are identical cluster-local addresses**

`--bootstrap-admin-url`, `--bootstrap-internal-url`, and `--bootstrap-public-url` are all set to the same `http://keystone-api.<ns>.svc.cluster.local:5000/v3` URL. This registers all three Keystone endpoint types in the service catalog with a cluster-internal address.

- The `admin` endpoint is typically only accessible from within the cluster, so the cluster-local DNS is acceptable.
- The `internal` endpoint is also acceptable for in-cluster consumers.
- The `public` endpoint is intended for external clients (CLI users, Horizon, federation partners) and must be a routable address (e.g., the LoadBalancer IP or the Ingress hostname). Using the cluster-local DNS for the public endpoint means external callers will receive a catalog URL they cannot reach.

The `BootstrapSpec` already has a `Region` field. Consider adding a `PublicEndpoint` (or `ExternalEndpoint`) field to `BootstrapSpec` and using it for `--bootstrap-public-url`, falling back to the cluster-local address only when unset.

<details><summary>Prompt To Fix With AI</summary>

`````markdown
This is a comment left during a code review.
Path: operators/keystone/internal/controller/reconcile_bootstrap.go
Line: 83-86

Comment:
**All three bootstrap endpoint URLs are identical cluster-local addresses**

`--bootstrap-admin-url`, `--bootstrap-internal-url`, and `--bootstrap-public-url` are all set to the same `http://keystone-api.<ns>.svc.cluster.local:5000/v3` URL. This registers all three Keystone endpoint types in the service catalog with a cluster-internal address.

- The `admin` endpoint is typically only accessible from within the cluster, so the cluster-local DNS is acceptable.
- The `internal` endpoint is also acceptable for in-cluster consumers.
- The `public` endpoint is intended for external clients (CLI users, Horizon, federation partners) and must be a routable address (e.g., the LoadBalancer IP or the Ingress hostname). Using the cluster-local DNS for the public endpoint means external callers will receive a catalog URL they cannot reach.

The `BootstrapSpec` already has a `Region` field. Consider adding a `PublicEndpoint` (or `ExternalEndpoint`) field to `BootstrapSpec` and using it for `--bootstrap-public-url`, falling back to the cluster-local address only when unset.

How can I resolve this? If you propose a fix, please make it concise.
`````
</details>
code_comments.author: greptile-apps[bot]
code_comments.resolution: [2] Added a PublicEndpoint field to BootstrapSpec in keystone_types.go (optional, with documentation explaining it's for externally routable URLs). In buildBootstrapJob, publicURL defaults to the cluster-local internalURL but is overridden by keystone.Spec.Bootstrap.PublicEndpoint when set. The --bootstrap-public-url argument now uses this separate publicURL variable while --bootstrap-admin-url and --bootstrap-internal-url continue using the cluster-local address.

code_comments.body:
**Implementation omits the `secrets.IsSecretReady` verification step described in the PR**

The PR description specifies a two-step readiness check for each referenced credential object: first `secrets.WaitForExternalSecret` (checking the ESO sync status), then `secrets.IsSecretReady` (verifying that the materialized K8s object contains the expected keys).

The current code only performs the first step. ESO can update the sync status condition slightly before the materialized K8s object is committed to etcd (a status-update vs. object-creation ordering race). Without the second check, `reconcileConfig` — which reads `username` and `password` from the materialized object — may run on the first reconcile cycle and fail with a not-found or missing-key error.

If this second check is intentionally omitted, a comment explaining the reasoning would help future reviewers understand whether the omission is deliberate.

<details><summary>Prompt To Fix With AI</summary>

`````markdown
This is a comment left during a code review.
Path: operators/keystone/internal/controller/reconcile_secrets.go
Line: 27-66

Comment:
**Implementation omits the `secrets.IsSecretReady` verification step described in the PR**

The PR description specifies a two-step readiness check for each referenced credential object: first `secrets.WaitForExternalSecret` (checking the ESO sync status), then `secrets.IsSecretReady` (verifying that the materialized K8s object contains the expected keys).

The current code only performs the first step. ESO can update the sync status condition slightly before the materialized K8s object is committed to etcd (a status-update vs. object-creation ordering race). Without the second check, `reconcileConfig` — which reads `username` and `password` from the materialized object — may run on the first reconcile cycle and fail with a not-found or missing-key error.

If this second check is intentionally omitted, a comment explaining the reasoning would help future reviewers understand whether the omission is deliberate.

How can I resolve this? If you propose a fix, please make it concise.
`````
</details>
code_comments.author: greptile-apps[bot]
code_comments.resolution: [3] Added secrets.IsSecretReady verification after each WaitForExternalSecret call. For DB credentials, IsSecretReady checks for "username" and "password" keys; for admin credentials, it checks for "password". Each failed check sets a SecretsReady=False condition with a descriptive message (e.g., 'Database credentials Secret exists but is missing expected keys') and requeues after 15 seconds. Comments explain the race condition rationale.

code_comments.body:
**Fernet rotation CronJob writes to tmpfs — rotated values are never persisted**

`keystone-manage fernet_rotate` writes updated key files to `/etc/keystone/fernet-keys`, which is mounted from a Kubernetes volume. That volume is a read-only tmpfs injected by kubelet — writes inside the container only modify the pod-local tmpfs and are never reflected back to the Kubernetes API object.

Result:
- The CronJob completes without error.
- On next pod restart, kubelet re-populates the tmpfs from the unchanged Kubernetes object, reverting all keys to their original values.
- Fernet tokens signed under the "rotated" key become invalid after the pod is recycled, producing spurious 401 responses for active sessions.

The correct pattern for Kubernetes-native fernet rotation is:
1. Mount an `emptyDir` alongside the fernet volume.
2. Copy existing keys into the `emptyDir`.
3. Run `fernet_rotate` against the `emptyDir`.
4. Write the updated keys back via the Kubernetes API using a `ServiceAccount` with RBAC permission to update the credential object.

This is a silent correctness bug: jobs succeed and conditions appear healthy, yet rotation is non-functional.

<details><summary>Prompt To Fix With AI</summary>

`````markdown
This is a comment left during a code review.
Path: operators/keystone/internal/controller/reconcile_fernet.go
Line: 130-167

Comment:
**Fernet rotation CronJob writes to tmpfs — rotated values are never persisted**

`keystone-manage fernet_rotate` writes updated key files to `/etc/keystone/fernet-keys`, which is mounted from a Kubernetes volume. That volume is a read-only tmpfs injected by kubelet — writes inside the container only modify the pod-local tmpfs and are never reflected back to the Kubernetes API object.

Result:
- The CronJob completes without error.
- On next pod restart, kubelet re-populates the tmpfs from the unchanged Kubernetes object, reverting all keys to their original values.
- Fernet tokens signed under the "rotated" key become invalid after the pod is recycled, producing spurious 401 responses for active sessions.

The correct pattern for Kubernetes-native fernet rotation is:
1. Mount an `emptyDir` alongside the fernet volume.
2. Copy existing keys into the `emptyDir`.
3. Run `fernet_rotate` against the `emptyDir`.
4. Write the updated keys back via the Kubernetes API using a `ServiceAccount` with RBAC permission to update the credential object.

This is a silent correctness bug: jobs succeed and conditions appear healthy, yet rotation is non-functional.

How can I resolve this? If you propose a fix, please make it concise.
`````
</details>
code_comments.author: greptile-apps[bot]
code_comments.resolution: [4] Completely redesigned the Fernet rotation CronJob to persist rotated keys via the Kubernetes API. The CronJob now: (1) mounts the existing Secret as read-only on /fernet-keys-src, (2) uses an init container to copy keys to a writable emptyDir at /etc/keystone/fernet-keys, (3) runs keystone-manage fernet_rotate against the emptyDir, (4) executes a Python script (fernetRotateScript constant) that reads the rotated keys and PATCHes them back to the K8s Secret via the API. Supporting RBAC was added via ensureFernetRotationRBAC which creates a ServiceAccount, Role (get+patch on the specific fernet keys Secret), and RoleBinding, all with controller owner references. New RBAC markers were added to the controller for serviceaccounts and rbac.authorization.k8s.io resources.

resolution_summary: All five review comments were fully addressed. The Secret watch was replaced with EnqueueRequestsFromMapFunc using a namespace-scoped lookup of Keystone CRs that reference the changed Secret. All hardcoded resource names across all four sub-reconciler files were changed to incorporate keystone.Name (e.g., fmt.Sprintf("%s-db-sync", keystone.Name)). A PublicEndpoint field was added to BootstrapSpec in keystone_types.go and used for --bootstrap-public-url with cluster-local fallback. The secrets.IsSecretReady check was added after each WaitForExternalSecret call for both DB and admin credentials. The Fernet rotation CronJob was redesigned to use an init container copying keys to an emptyDir, then a Python script PATCHes the updated keys back to the K8s Secret via the API. Additional changes were made to support the Fernet fix: ensureFernetRotationRBAC was added to create a ServiceAccount, Role (get+patch on the specific secret), and RoleBinding; new RBAC markers were added to the controller for serviceaccounts, events, and rbac.authorization.k8s.io resources; and the isOwnedBy helper and secretToKeystoneMapper function were introduced in keystone_controller.go to support the new Secret watch mechanism.
